title: Salted toffee popcorn
subtitle: Freshly popped corn coated in a buttery toffee and baked to a crisp
excerpt: Freshly popped corn tossed in a hot butter toffee, scattered on trays, and baked until the coating sets hard and glossy. Finished with a generous scatter of flaky salt.
sourceNotes: Toffee-coated popcorn (caramel corn) is a sweet with roots in American street food of the late nineteenth century, appearing in commercial form from the 1890s. Cracker Jack, the original brand, was sold at the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago. The baked toffee popcorn method, baking the coated popcorn to dry and set the toffee coating rather than simply coating and cooling, became the standard home recipe in the mid-twentieth century. The salted version follows more recent British and American artisan sweet-making traditions. This recipe follows the classic baked toffee corn approach.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily using two large roasting tins.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in an airtight container for up to 1 week. Moisture softens the coating, so seal well.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 120°C. Low and slow dries the toffee coating without burning.

glossaryTerms.definition: The toffee for popcorn is cooked with bicarbonate of soda added at the end, which causes it to foam slightly and become lighter in texture. This aerated toffee coats the popcorn more evenly and produces a crisper, less slab-like coating than plain toffee. The bicarbonate also introduces a very slight caramel depth.

After coating, the popcorn is spread on lined trays and baked at a low temperature (140°C) for 30 to 40 minutes, stirring every 10 minutes. The low heat drives off moisture from the toffee and sets it to a hard, dry gloss. Without this step, the coating remains slightly sticky.

content.text: The toffee will be around 150°C when you pour it over the popcorn. Work quickly and keep children away from the pan and bowl.
is lightened with bicarbonate of soda for a crisper result. The coating only fully sets and loses its stickiness after
— skipping the oven step produces chewy, sticky popcorn rather than the crisp result.

Heat the oil in a large, deep saucepan over high heat. Add 3 test kernels and cover. When they pop, add the remaining kernels in a single layer, cover, and reduce to medium-high heat. Shake the pan gently every 30 seconds. When popping slows to one pop every 3 to 4 seconds, remove from heat. Transfer to a very large bowl, discarding any unpopped kernels. Preheat the oven to 140°C conventional.

Melt the butter, brown sugar, and golden syrup together in a saucepan over medium heat, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Bring to a boil and cook without stirring for 4 minutes. Remove from heat and stir in the bicarbonate of soda — the mixture will foam. Immediately pour over the popcorn and toss quickly with two large spoons to coat as evenly as possible.

Spread the coated popcorn in as thin a layer as possible across two lined roasting tins. Bake for 30 to 40 minutes, stirring and breaking up clumps every 10 minutes. The coating should be dry and set (not sticky) when done. Scatter with flaky salt immediately as it comes out of the oven. Cool completely before breaking into pieces and storing.
